claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

Let me start by reading the official guidance to ensure I provide you with accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now let me help you determine your eligibility.

To start, **do you have any children that you're responsible for?** If yes, how many children are you asking about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit. I have one child - my son Alex. He's 8 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have one child, Alex, who is 8 years old.

To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask you a few questions:

**Do you currently live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. That's helpful.

**Do you live with Alex, or does he live with someone else?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't live with me. He lives with his mum.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand. Alex lives with his mum rather than with you.

**Do you contribute financially towards Alex's upkeep?** For example, do you pay for things like food, clothes, pocket money, or contribute towards his accommodation? If yes, approximately how much do you contribute per week or per month?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do contribute towards Alex's upkeep. I help pay for his clothes, school dinners, and bits and pieces he needs.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To assess your eligibility properly, I need to understand the amount you're contributing.

**Approximately how much do you contribute towards Alex's upkeep per week or per month?** (This helps determine if your contribution meets the threshold for Child Benefit eligibility when a child lives with someone else.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I contribute £30 a week towards his upkeep.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that detail. 

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex** (such as his mum or another person)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, his mum isn't claiming it - nobody else is getting Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that.

Just to confirm a few final details:

**Is Alex in any of the following situations:**
- In hospital or residential care for an extended period?
- In local authority care?
- Working 24 hours or more per week?
- Claiming benefits in his own right (like Universal Credit or Employment Support Allowance)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, none of those apply. He's not in hospital or care, he doesn't work, and he doesn't get any benefits in his own name. He's just a regular 8-year-old living with his mum.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on what you've told me:
- Alex is 8 years old (under 16)
- You live in the UK
- Alex lives with his mum, not with you
- You contribute £30 per week towards his upkeep
- Nobody else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- Alex is not in hospital, care, or any other special circumstances

According to the official guidance, when a child lives with someone else, you can still qualify for Child Benefit if you contribute towards the child's upkeep by at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment.

The current Child Benefit rate for one child is £25.60 per week (for the eldest/only child). Since you're contributing £30 per week, which is **more than** the Child Benefit amount, you would meet this requirement.

However, there's an important point: **only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child**. Since Alex's mum could also potentially claim (as Alex lives with her), you and Alex's mum would need to agree between yourselves who should claim. If you cannot agree, HMRC will decide who gets it based on who has the primary responsibility for the child.

**Have you discussed with Alex's mum about who should claim the Child Benefit?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh, I see. No, we haven't really discussed it properly. So even though I'm contributing more than the Child Benefit amount, because he lives with her, we'd need to sort out between us who should claim it?

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Yes, that's exactly right. Even though you're contributing more than the Child Benefit amount (which does make you potentially eligible), because only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child, you and Alex's mum would need to decide between yourselves who will claim it.

Since Alex lives with his mum, she would typically be considered to have primary responsibility for him, which means HMRC would likely award it to her if you both claimed and couldn't agree. However, if she doesn't want to claim it and you both agree that you should claim it instead, then you could do so.

**Would you like me to provide my final assessment of your eligibility based on the information you've given me?** Or do you have any other questions about your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, please give me your final assessment. I'd like to understand whether I'm eligible or not.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Final Assessment

**You ARE eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why:

✓ **Age requirement met**: Alex is 8 years old, which is under 16

✓ **UK residency**: You live in the UK

✓ **Financial contribution**: Even though Alex lives with his mum, you contribute £30 per week towards his upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit rate of £25.60 per week. This meets the requirement for claiming when a child lives elsewhere

✓ **No competing claim**: Nobody else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex

✓ **No disqualifying circumstances**: Alex is not in care, hospital, working 24+ hours, or claiming benefits in his own right

**Important consideration**: Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child. Since Alex lives with his mum, she could also potentially claim. If she decides she wants to claim Child Benefit too, you would need to agree between yourselves who will claim it. If you cannot agree, HMRC would decide based on who has primary responsibility (typically the person the child lives with).

As long as his mum doesn't claim or agrees that you should claim, you can proceed with your application for Child Benefit for Alex.

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), which meets the basic age requirement for Child Benefit. Although Alex lives with his mum rather than with you, the rules state you can still qualify for Child Benefit if you contribute towards the child's upkeep by at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment. You contribute £30 per week, which exceeds the current Child Benefit rate of £25.60 per week for one child. You live in the UK and no one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Alex is not in hospital, care, working, or claiming benefits in his own right. Therefore, you meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex. However, it's important to note that only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child. Since Alex lives with his mum, if she also wanted to claim, you would need to agree between yourselves who will claim. If you cannot agree, HMRC would decide based on who has primary responsibility (typically the person the child lives with). As long as his mum agrees not to claim or doesn't claim herself, you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for his 8-year-old son Alex. He meets all the criteria: Alex is under 16, the claimant lives in the UK, and he contributes £30 per week towards Alex's upkeep (which exceeds the Child Benefit amount of £25.60 per week), satisfying the requirement for claiming when a child lives elsewhere. No one else is currently claiming for Alex. The key consideration is that only one person can claim Child Benefit per child, so if Alex's mum (who Alex lives with) also wanted to claim, they would need to agree who claims, or HMRC would decide. Assuming no competing claim, the claimant is eligible.

If you cannot agree, HMRC would decide based on who has primary responsibility (typically the person the child lives with). As long as his mum agrees not to claim or doesn't claim herself, you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

No one else is currently claiming for Alex. The key consideration is that only one person can claim Child Benefit per child, so if Alex's mum (who Alex lives with) also wanted to claim, they would need to agree who claims, or HMRC would decide.
